summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the 'Don't Read The Comments' movement, maintaining that comments are essential to blogs and provide crowdsourced knowledge despite their noise. He contends that toxic comment sections are the fault of website owners who fail to moderate, not an inevitable feature of online discourse. The solution isn't abandoning comments but building software that enables community self-governance—a lesson he learned from Stack Overflow. He announces that Coding Horror is switching to Discourse, the open-source discussion platform he created to help communities moderate themselves at scale.
key_points: Comments produce crowdsourced knowledge in aggregate and should be read alongside the main content, like following Wikipedia citations
If your website is full of toxic commenters, it's your fault as the site owner—moderation is a responsibility, not an option
Professional moderation doesn't scale: skilled moderators are rare, expensive, prone to burnout, and create governance challenges
The only sustainable moderation is community self-governance, which requires software designed to encourage positive behavior and mitigate negative behavior
Discourse was built to handle civilized discourse principles automatically, even if owners walk away
money_quotes: A blog without comments is not a blog.
If you want the complete picture, in the same way that you should follow all those little citation links in Wikipedia articles, you should read the comments.
If your website is full of assholes, it's your fault.
If you are unwilling to moderate your online community, you don't deserve to have an online community.
The only form of moderation that scales with the community is the community itself.
It's always a people problem. This is absolutely true. But it's also true that software can profoundly affect people's behavior.
Civilization begins with software that actively works to help you create safe environments for having reasonable conversations with other human beings.
key_insight: Toxic online communities aren't inevitable—they're a design failure, and the solution is building software that empowers communities to govern themselves rather than relying on unsustainable professional moderation.
